data.title: Don’t dismiss the possibility of a peaceful transition in Venezuela
data.description:
Critics of the Trump administration’s increasingly militarized posture toward Venezuela are right to highlight the perils of any operation aimed at regime change. Yet focusing solely on calamitous scenarios risks overlooking the potential for a successful and relatively peaceful democratic transition in Venezuela.

There are valid reasons for concern. The operation is directed by a national security team composed largely of ill-equipped figures hired for their loyalty to Donald Trump. Its most visible result so far has been strikes on small boats allegedly transporting narcotics — actions that even some Republican lawmakers question — and the seizure of a Venezuelan oil tanker earlier this week. 

Moreover, the historical record of U.S. interventions to topple governments is hardly instructive or reassuring. The few successes targeted small, vulnerable countries like Grenada and Panama. The most notorious case, in Iraq, unleashed instability that initially undermined rather than advanced U.S. national security interests.

Still, policymakers should weigh not only the risks of failure in Venezuela but also the conditions that could make success conceivable.

At least six factors distinguish Venezuela from cases where regime change worsened conditions. First, Venezuela retains a democratic tradition — one that has not been fully extinguished by strongman Nicolas Maduro’s regime. Despite threats and harassment, political actors and civil society organizations continue to operate and defend what remains of democratic space.

Second, unlike Iraq or Libya, Venezuela is not fractured by ethnic, religious or tribal cleavages. Third, Venezuelans are politically united in opposition to Maduro. They voted overwhelmingly in last year’s presidential contest for opposition leader Edmundo Gonzalez, only to see Maduro steal the vote and repress dissent.

Fourth, the Venezuelan opposition is largely unified behind Nobel Laureate Maria Corina Machado, the country’s most popular political figure by far, according to polls.

Fifth, there are professionals inside the country and among the 8 million in exile capable of rebuilding political institutions and reviving the economy. Oil production and foreign investment would also get a boost following an expected lifting of U.S. sanctions once a democratic government is in place.

Sixth and finally, the opposition understands that the socialist movement founded by Maduro’s predecessor, Hugo Chavez, commands millions of adherents despite Chavismo’s disastrous misrule, and that that movement should have a place in a democratic Venezuela.

Machado’s central message the last couple years has been the need to reunite Venezuela and its families, at home and abroad. Although one of the regime’s most strident critics, Machado, who fled Venezuela this week after more than a year in hiding, is pragmatic. She understands that any democratic transition will require dialogue with regime figures and concessions to senior civilian and military leaders.

The opposition appreciates that a balance between accountability for abuses and national reconciliation will be required. And it accepts that there could be interim steps, including a coalition arrangement, between Maduro’s departure and the assumption of power by an elected democratic government.

These factors will help but not guarantee a peaceful transition. If anything gives Trump pause from ordering direct military action against Venezuela, it is likely fear not just of failing to dislodge Maduro but of succeeding and creating a power vacuum that obligates U.S. troops to try to maintain order. 

During Trump’s first term, a U.S. government war game concluded that chaos and violence would likely ensue within Venezuela if Maduro were ousted, as security forces, political factions and guerrilla groups vied for control.

The administration is hoping that psychological pressure — the deployment of massive firepower, leaks about “covert” operations and threats that Maduro’s “days are numbered” — will prod him to negotiate his exit or provoke the military to depose him and claim the U.S. bounty of up to $50 million for his capture.  

During a recent phone call, Trump reportedly offered Maduro safe passage for him, his wife and his son if he resigned right away, per anonymous sources.

But even if the United States dislodges Maduro without direct strikes on Venezuela, a peaceful democratic transition would not be assured. 

The Venezuelan armed forces — the linchpin sustaining the regime’s power — do not trust Machado, and well-armed pro-regime militias roam freely, intimidating and coercing civilians. Colombian guerrilla groups are active inside Venezuela, and criminal organizations operate with autonomy. 

A stable transition depends, therefore, on the military’s willingness to accept civilian, democratic rule and confront irregular forces, including those with which it has previously collaborated.

To encourage that shift, members of Machado’s inner circle told me the opposition has privately floated to the military a set of assurances and incentives, including respect for its institutional role and some form of amnesty. 

Washington should reinforce those messages, while conveying it would support a new democratic government’s efforts to secure the resource-strapped armed forces with improved pay and modernized equipment.

What’s the likelihood of success along the lines of this hopeful scenario? Probably no greater than that of Syrian President Bashar Assad suddenly falling from power and his successor, a former al Qaeda militant, largely containing sectarian tensions and terrorist groups while engaging constructively with the United States and Israel.

Foreign policy scenario planning must always anticipate the worst outcomes. But with skillful, pragmatic and well-intentioned actors, events can surprise on the upside. 

Mark Feierstein, a non-resident senior advisor at the Center for Strategic and International Studies, served as special assistant to President Barack Obama and senior director for Western Hemisphere Affairs on the National Security Council.

data.title: GOP leaders weighing vote on ObamaCare extension
data.description:
House Republican leaders are considering allowing an amendment vote to extend ObamaCare enhanced subsidies, a move designed to appease moderate Republicans who have been warning about the electoral impacts of letting them expire at the end of the year.

In a closed-door meeting in the Capitol Friday morning, GOP leaders huddled with various groups of Republicans to hash out their strategy on the enhanced tax credits under the Affordable Care Act (ACA), which benefit more than 20 million people. 

Leaving the meeting, party leaders said no final decisions have been made. But the conversation focused largely on the possibility of staging a vote on an extension of the ObamaCare subsidies as an amendment to the larger GOP health care package, which is expected to get a vote next week. 

“We just have a couple final pieces to work out, but we are narrowing it and getting really close,” Rep Steve Scalise (R-La.) told reporters after the meeting. 

Asked if there would be a vote on an ObamaCare amendment, Scalise said it’s still in the works. 

“That’s still being discussed, but it was heavily discussed. And we’ve just got to work a few things out,” he said.

Any such amendment vote would be designed to give moderate Republicans political cover, but would have little chance of becoming law. It’s unclear if Democrats would support such an amendment, and even if it was adopted, it would be a poison pill for the larger GOP health care plan, eroding support from conservatives.

“We had a very productive meeting. We’re coming to the final points of the plan, and we’re excited about what we’re going to do next week,” Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) said.

The health care package GOP leaders are putting together is set to contain items on which Republicans have broad consensus, such as expansion of health savings accounts and reforms to the pharmacy benefit manager industry. Since Republicans are intensely divided on the subsidies, the underlying legislation is not set to include an extension.

The expiring subsidies have posed a dilemma for Republican leaders, who are being squeezed between centrist Republicans clamoring to extend the subsidies and a broad portion of Republicans — including members of the hardline House Freedom Caucus and conservative Republican Study Committee — who want to see the benefits lapse. Johnson has repeatedly criticized the subsidies.

If Congress doesn’t tackle the issue this month, health costs are expected to spike for roughly 22 million Americans.

There are currently four competing health care proposals competing for attention in the lower chamber: The Republicans’ partisan package; a Democratic bill to extend the ACA subsidies for three years with no other changes; and two bipartisan proposals that would extend the tax credits for a shorter span — one for two years, the other for one — while applying new limits on eligibility to favor lower-income beneficiaries. 

Rep. Brian Fitzpatrick (R-Pa.), the leader of one of those bipartisan plans, was in the Friday morning meeting.

The Republican bill has little chance of passing through the Senate, where 60 votes are needed to overcome a Democratic filibuster. The Democrats’ three-year extension also has long odds, since it failed in the Senate on Thursday with support from just 51 senators — nine shy of passage. 

Supporters of the remaining bipartisan bills are pushing discharge petitions to force votes on those proposals, which have already gained the support of more than 10 Republicans. Most Democrats, however, are waiting to see what Johnson does before they decide whether to endorse one or both of those petitions.

House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) said Democrats are still “actively reviewing” those petitions and will weigh in further “early next week.”

data.title: Democrats release new Epstein photos showing Trump, Bill Clinton
data.description:
Democrats on Friday released 19 new photos from convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein’s estate, showing the deceased financier alongside President Trump and former President Bill Clinton.

“These disturbing images raise even more questions about Epstein and his relationships with some of the most powerful men in the world,” Democrats on the House Oversight Committee wrote in a post on the social platform X.

“Time to end this White House cover-up. Release the files!” the statement continues.

Democrats said they had obtained 95,000 new photos from the Epstein estate.

The 19 they released online include images of Epstein and several notable figures.

The images tweeted by Oversight Democrats included a black and white photo of Trump with six women. The women’s faces are covered with black boxes to prevent their identification.

Another photo shows condoms with a cartoon image of Trump on each package along with the words “I’m HUUUUGE!”

Trump has long denied any wrongdoing in connection to Epstein and has alleged Democrats have undertaken a “witch hunt” against him by promoting his ties to the millionaire. 

Trump and Epstein knew each other from their days in New York and were known to socialize, but had a falling-out that Trump has blamed on Epstein seeking to steal employees from his Mar-a-Lago spa.

The House and Senate earlier this fall after a long battle approved legislation calling on the Department of Justice to release all of its files on Epstein.

The photos released by Democrats shows a number of famous people with Epstein, including Steve Bannon, the former Trump adviser, as well as Clinton, filmmaker Woody Allen, attorney Alan Dershowitz and Microsoft founder Bill Gates.

The photo of Clinton shows him posing with Epstein and Ghislaine Maxwell and two other people. Clinton has also denied links to Epstein’s underground sex trafficking ring.

data.title: Does religious liberty exist in prison? It depends.
data.description:
Louisiana prison guards handcuffed Damon Landor to a chair, held him down, and shaved his dreadlocks — an act that blatantly violated his Rastafarian faith and the rights he maintained in jail to practice his religion.

Now, the Supreme Court seems ready to let those guards completely off the hook, just like every lower court to consider Landor’s case. This is because, although there is a federal law designed to protect prisoners’ religious rights, many courts have found that it does not allow people to seek money from the officials who violate those rights.

But behind this debate over monetary compensation, Landor’s story exposes how the system of prison-transfers quietly undermines justice before a lawsuit can ever be filed. 

Landor’s story began with a brief five-month sentence in 2020. For most of that time, he was in two facilities that respected his faith and allowed him to wear his hair long or keep it under a “rastacap.” With just three weeks left in his sentence, Landor was transferred to a new facility. Even after presenting prison officials with a federal court decision saying that Louisiana could not force Rastafarians to cut their hair, prison guards held him down and shaved his head anyway.

When Landor got out and sued, he wanted more than just money. Like many individuals who file lawsuits against prisons, Landor requested an order for declaratory and injunctive relief. In other words, Landor wanted an order saying that his rights had been violated, as well as an order stopping prison officials from doing the same thing again.

The district court ruled that because Landor had already been released from prison, it could not grant either. And courts often take that view, reasoning that once a person is transferred or released, there are no ongoing conditions left to fix, even when the violation itself is clear. 

This makes prison transfers uniquely powerful, and Landor’s case is a perfect example of why. As long as money is not available as a remedy to individuals like Landor, claims against prison officials are “totally meaningless” — precisely as Landor’s lawyers have argued. If the only relief a victim can obtain is a court order instructing an official to not do or stop doing something, then a prison official can work around that by simply transferring the individual in question to a different facility. 

Indeed, that is precisely how prison transfers quietly erase justice, and this exact pattern already shapes prison rights litigation nationwide.

Moving a prisoner from one facility to another effectively kills most lawsuits before they start. As a result, the only claims that often survive are those seeking money. As Landor knows all too well, even these are difficult to maintain. Courts have severely restricted when and under what circumstances people in prison can receive money for what they experience — even those who experience something that is obviously illegal.

For Landor and many others, that leaves no meaningful path to justice. Even though lower courts emphatically condemned what happened to Landor, they still held that the guards who violated his faith would face no legal repercussions at all. Based on oral arguments, the Supreme Court seems poised to agree. 

The Constitution does not stop at the prison gate, but it appears our remedies often do. Landor’s case is a warning that if courts keep closing the few remaining paths to accountability, prisoners’ rights will exist only on paper. If a state can violate a person’s faith, transfer them somewhere else and face no consequences at all, then justice isn’t just delayed — it is erased. 

Jessica Williams Schnelker teaches at Indiana University Robert H. McKinney School of Law, where her research examines how the design of legal rules and procedures affects how people navigate the justice system.

data.title: How to partner AI with human compassion in suicide prevention
data.description:
Too often, there’s a new story about the challenges that people face when relying on artificial intelligence chatbots for advice and support. Tragically, several involve deaths by suicide of teens and young adults who used AI chatbots as “therapists” — in California, Colorado, Florida and Texas, to name a few.

The stories compel us to wrestle with a painful truth: While AI tools are accessible, trusting bots with suicide prevention is a gamble that too many people engage in. More than 1 million active users of ChatGPT, for example, have “conversations that include explicit indicators of potential suicidal planning or intent.”

In response, a new California law mandates that people who engage in extensive conversations with AI chatbots are reminded every three hours that they are not talking to a real person. 

Chatbot platform Character.ai took one step further in October, announcing that its services would not be available to anyone under the age of 18. The restrictions improve on the parental controls introduced in September by OpenAI, ChatGPT’s parent company.

But the problem starts before the first message is sent. The stark reality is that almost 60 percent of youth who die by suicide never saw a mental health professional. Suicide remains the second leading cause of death in young people in the U.S. Access to a firearm triples these risks.

Suicidal thoughts are often shrouded in secrecy. Research suggests that more than half of people contemplating suicide tell no one and many fear involuntary hospitalization. Even when young people find their way into a clinic, consistent access to evidence-based care is far from guaranteed

Because the U.S. health care system does not adequately serve the needs of many young people with mental health concerns, they are turning to other means of support. AI chatbots have been available for decades, but the technology and interactivity have become more sophisticated and personalized. Some AI “therapists” even have names.

As mental health professionals, we get the appeal. Chatbots are always accessible. They don’t cost much. They don’t judge. Their responses come off as caring and concerned. If you’re lying awake at 2 a.m., feeling alone, it can feel easier to type into a chatbox than to wait months for an appointment or risk embarrassment by asking for help in person.

AI is quick. It provides the illusion of privacy. It’s available when no one else is, and there is no need to obtain parental consent.

Suicide risk guidelines and protocols have been programmed into chatbots, such as de-escalation approaches and safety planning. AI can easily pick up on words like “hopeless” or “suicidal.” But assessing suicide risk is often more nuanced.

Think about a doctor trying to understand chest pain. Sometimes it’s indigestion. Sometimes it’s a heart attack. You don’t want a chatbot making that call. The same goes for assessing risk for suicide. A passing thought of despair isn’t the same as someone ready to act, and algorithms can’t reliably tell the difference.

Suicide prevention isn’t just about talking. It’s about connection, context and accountability. And those are things that machine learning can’t always give you.

In suicide prevention, the small human gestures matter most. A clinician noticing a pause before an answer. A friend who insists on staying until morning. A teacher who makes a phone call because something didn’t feel right in class. These things save lives. A chatbot can’t tilt its head, lean forward, or look you in the eye.

We’re not saying AI has no place in mental health care. It could help with reminders, track symptoms or give people structured exercises between therapy visits. That’s useful. But AI should support care, not replace it. When someone’s life is at risk, the only safe path is human connection with trained, accountable professionals.

What we need is insurers to cover real, evidence-based treatment. We need more clinicians, especially in rural and underserved communities, trained in best practices for suicide prevention. We need to chip away at stigma so people feel safe asking for help. And we need regulations to make sure AI tools have guardrails in life of death situations.

The most recent data available shows that almost 50,000 Americans died by suicide in 2023, so our ability to meet young people where they are, whether it be on social media and ChatGPT, has never been more important. 

But there is an urgent need for partnerships with expert suicide prevention researchers to understand how to improve identification and understand which users could be harmed by AI chats and under what conditions.  

AI organizations must not be left to design suicide prevention tools in a vacuum. They need to partner with suicide prevention researchers and clinicians to test their products, understand which users could be harmed and build safeguards grounded in evidence. 

Without that collaboration, the risks will outweigh the benefits for users, especially youth, at risk for suicide.

Holly C. Wilcox, Ph.D., and Paul S. Nestadt, MD, lead the Johns Hopkins Center for Suicide Prevention.

data.title: Trump to honor members of ‘Miracle on Ice’ team Friday
data.description:
President Trump will honor members of the 1980 Olympic U.S. men’s hockey team at the White House on Friday, awarding them with congressional medals.

Trump will host members of the team, which triumphed over the Soviet Union in the famous “Miracle on Ice” game in February of 1980, during an Oval Office event scheduled for 3 p.m.

The House and Senate passed legislation earlier this year to award members of the team with Congressional Gold Medals. Among those expected to be in attendance are former captain Mike Eruzione and former goaltender Jim Craig.

CBS News first reported on the event.

The U.S. men’s hockey team was a significant underdog to the Soviet Union team in the 1980 game, which took place in Lake Placid, N.Y. The U.S. team won 4-3 and went on to secure the gold medal in their next game.

Trump has frequently attached himself to sports and popular athletes since the start of his second term in January. He has attended the Super Bowl, the Daytona 500, multiple Ultimate Fighting Championship (UFC) events and a Washington Commanders game.

He has also hosted prominent athletes at the White House, including golfer Bryson DeChambeau and soccer superstar Cristiano Ronaldo.

data.title: Watch live: Jeffries gives remarks amid health care, redistricting battle
data.description:
House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) will speak with reporters Friday morning after senators struck down Democrats’ effort to extend Affordable Care Act (ACA) subsidies, which are set to expire at the end of the year.

The Senate took up dueling health care plans on Thursday, and both failed to garner the support needed to advance. Democrats pushed for a 3-year extension of the ACA tax credits as health care premiums are set to rise, while Republicans’ plan centered on promoting health savings accounts to pay out-of-pocket costs.

The expiring subsidies were the central issue at play during the 43-day government shutdown.

Friday’s press conference also comes a day after the GOP took another blow in its redistricting fight after GOP legislators in Indiana blocked a new map that was backed by President Trump.

The press conference is scheduled to begin at 11 a.m. EST.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: Walmart, Sam’s Club roll out new ‘Eye Bot’ kiosks
data.description:
(WHTM) — Walmart is using Pennsylvania as a test market for a new way to provide eye exams.

The retailer is rolling out new “Eye Bot” kiosks at 16 Pennsylvania locations — 12 Walmart vision centers and four Sam’s Club Optical Centers — which they say folks can use to get an eye exam in 15 minutes.

“In areas where access might be limited, tools like this help our teams serve customers and members more efficiently,” said Jason Wong, Regional Health & Wellness Director for Walmart, covering Pennsylvania.

“It enables clinicians to focus on the comprehensive care they’re uniquely trained to provide,” he added.

The exam costs $25 at Walmart and is free for Sam’s Club members.

data.title: New restrictions on what SNAP can buy in 2026: Here’s what to know
data.description:
(NEXSTAR) – Six more states are adding restrictions to what SNAP beneficiaries can buy with their monthly benefits, the federal government announced this week.

Hawaii, Missouri, North Dakota, South Dakota, Virginia and Tennessee are joining 12 other states in adding waivers that will redefine what types of food can be purchased with SNAP dollars in 2026. The changes vary slightly state to state, but in most cases make soda, candy or energy drinks off limits.

“With these new waivers, we are empowering states to lead, protecting our children from the dangers of highly-processed foods, and moving one step closer to the President’s promise to Make America Healthy Again,” said Secretary of Agriculture Brooke Rollins in a press release.

The waivers will be implemented at different points throughout the year. Florida, for example, is banning the use of federal food aid to purchase soda, energy drinks, candy and prepared desserts starting Jan. 1. In Missouri, recipients have a similar measure taking effect in October.

Iowa is taking a more roundabout approach to restricting the purchase of unhealthy foods with SNAP benefits. Starting in January, the state is restricting SNAP purchases to only non-taxable food items. The state allows for sales tax on candy, prepared food and soft drinks, which means they’ll no longer be approved for SNAP purchases.

See the interactive map below to learn more about the new limits coming to 18 states in 2026:

The new bans on soda and candy aren’t the only restrictions on how people can spend their SNAP benefits. Nonfood items (like soap or paper products), alcohol, tobacco, vitamins, medications, and hot foods are all already off limits in every state.

The Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP), formerly known as the Food Stamp Program, helps approximately 1 in 8 Americans afford groceries. Beneficiaries receive monthly benefits on an EBT card, which they can use to shop at approved retailers.

SNAP has been in the spotlight this year as the One Big Beautiful Bill cut funding for the program over the summer. The bill also added new restrictions on who could receive food aid based on employment and immigration status. Then, during the record-long government shutdown, the Trump administration announced November benefits wouldn’t go out, triggering confusion and a legal battle.

The battle over SNAP isn’t over yet, either. States are still on the hook to rein in their error rates or face further funding cuts. The Trump administration is also threatening to withhold benefits from states that don’t hand over data on participants, including their immigration statuses.

data.title: Court blocks immediate release of Chicago-area immigrants detained in federal crackdown
data.description:
A federal appeals court on Thursday blocked the immediate release of Chicago-area immigrants detained in the Trump administration’s federal crackdown on the Windy City. 

The 7th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals ruled 2-1 to extend a consent decree, which allows federal authorities to make warrantless arrests, according to The Asociated Press.

A group of 600 immigrants detained in “Operation Midway Blitz” were awaiting release after District Judge Jeffrey Cummings issued a November ruling ordering authorities to give them bond, per the wire. 

However, the court of appeals said Cummings needed to assess each case individually citing the consent decree, which “carefully maps out what the district judge can or cannot order.”

The panel of judges did acknowledge that immigration officials were wrong to subject all individual arrestees to mandatory detention.

“We will work tirelessly to ensure that people who were unlawfully arrested will be able to return to their families and communities as soon as possible,” said Keren Zwick with the National Immigrant Justice Center.

Around 4,000 people were arrested during the Trump administration’s Chicago immigration operation. 

Judges in other states including Colorado have ruled against warrantless arrests of immigrants that are not the target of ongoing immigration enforcement plans.
